title: Psalms 94
subtitle: The LORD Is a God Who Avenges

sections.header: Verses 1–11 — “How Long Will the Wicked Be Jubilant?”

heb.paragraph: "The LORD is a God who avenges (nəqāmōṭ). O God who avenges, shine forth" (v.1). An appeal for divine justice. The wicked pour out arrogant words (v.4), crush God’s people (v.5), slay the widow and foreigner and murder the fatherless (v.6). They say "The LORD does not see" (v.7). The psalmist responds: "Does he who fashioned the ear not hear? Does he who formed the eye not see?" (v.9). The Creator of senses is not senseless.

notes.note: "O LORD, the God who avenges, shine forth. Rise up, Judge of the earth; pay back the proud what they deserve." Justice delayed is not justice denied. The request: make your justice visible.
"They crush your people; they slay the widow and murder the fatherless. They say, ‘The LORD does not see.’" The accusation: God is blind. The victims: the most vulnerable.
"Does he who fashioned the ear not hear? Does he who formed the eye not see?" The argument from creation: the maker of ears hears; the maker of eyes sees. God’s perception exceeds all he created.

refs.note: Vengeance belonging to God, 'I will repay,' directly quotes this psalm's theology.
The Lord judging his people and vengeance being his cites this tradition.
Martyrs crying 'how long until you avenge our blood?' echoes the psalm's plea.

hist.context: Psalm 94 calls on the divine Judge to repay the proud and rescue the oppressed. The description of wicked rulers who crush God's people, kill widows and orphans (vv.5-6), represents covenant violation. The question 'how long will the wicked be jubilant?' (v.3) expresses the righteous community's frustration with unpunished evil.

sections.header: Verses 12–23 — “Blessed Is the One You Discipline”

heb.paragraph: "Blessed is the one you discipline (yāsar), LORD, the one you teach from your law" (v.12). The turn: discipline is a sign of love, not abandonment. "The LORD will not reject his people; he will never forsake his inheritance" (v.14). "When I said, ‘My foot is slipping,’ your unfailing love, LORD, supported me" (v.18). The psalm closes with confidence: "The LORD will pay them back for their sins and destroy them for their wickedness" (v.23).

notes.note: "Blessed is the one you discipline. For the LORD will not reject his people; he will never forsake his inheritance." Discipline is proof of relationship, not evidence of rejection.

"Unless the LORD had given me help, I would soon have dwelt in the silence of death. When I said, ‘My foot is slipping,’ your love supported me. When anxiety was great within me, your consolation brought me joy." The personal testimony: slipping foot caught by ḥesed; anxious mind consoled by God’s comfort.
"The LORD has become my fortress, my God the rock in whom I take refuge. He will repay them for their sins." Fortress, rock, refuge — the standard trilogy. Justice will come.

refs.note: Paul quotes Psalm 94:11 in 1 Cor 3:20: 'the Lord knows that the thoughts of the wise are futile.'
The Lord disciplines those he loves develops the psalm's blessed-discipline theology.
Not despising the LORD's discipline parallels the psalm's beatitude on correction.

hist.context: The beatitude 'blessed is the one you discipline' (v.12) transforms suffering into education. The LORD does not reject his people or abandon his inheritance (v.14). The rhetorical questions — 'does he who fashioned the ear not hear?' (v.9) — demolish any notion of divine ignorance. The Judge of earth will act.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: "O LORD, the God who avenges, shine forth…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVTake notice, you senseless ones among the people; you fools, when will you become wise?ESVUnderstand, O dullest of the people! Fools, when will you be wise?NIVThey say, “The Lord does not see; the God of Jacob takes no notice.”ESVand they say, \u201cThe LORD does not see; the God of Jacob does not perceive.\u201d

rec.text: ""O LORD, the God who avenges, shine forth. Rise up, Judge of the earth; pay back the proud what they deserve." Justice delayed is not justice denied. The request: make your justice visible."

rows.label: Does God See? — 94:1–11
rows.text: "They say, ‘The LORD does not see.’" But: does the ear-maker not hear? The eye-maker not see?

hebtext.note: "The LORD is a God who avenges (nəqāmōṭ). O God who avenges, shine forth" (v.1). An appeal for divine justice. The wicked pour out arrogant words (v.4), crush God’s people (v.5), slay the widow and foreigner and murder the fatherless (v.6). They say "The LORD does not see" (v.7). The psalmist responds: "Does he who fashioned the ear not hear? Does he who formed the eye not see?" (v.9). The Creator of senses is not senseless.

"Blessed is the one you discipline (yāsar), LORD, the one you teach from your law" (v.12). The turn: discipline is a sign of love, not abandonment. "The LORD will not reject his people; he will never forsake his inheritance" (v.14). "When I said, ‘My foot is slipping,’ your unfailing love, LORD, supported me" (v.18). The psalm closes with confidence: "The LORD will pay them back for their sins and destroy them for their wickedness" (v.23).

themes.note: Justice peaks: the Creator of senses perceives all injustice. Mercy: slipping foot caught, anxious heart consoled. Judgment: God who avenges.

prayer_prompt: The God who avenges takes note of oppression. Consider asking God about the oppression you've witnessed or experienced — and what justice you're trusting Him to bring.
